en.extract:
The Bismarck monarch (Symposiachrus verticalis), formerly known as the black-tailed monarch, is a species of bird in the family Monarchidae. It is endemic to the Bismarck Archipelago of Papua New Guinea. Its natural habitat is subtropical or tropical moist lowland forests.


Taxonomy and systematics
This species was originally placed in the genus Monarcha until moved to Symposiachrus in 2009.
Two subspecies are recognized-

Black-tailed monarch, Symposiachrus verticalis verticalis Sclater, 1877- Bismarck Archipelago
Djaul monarch, Symposiachrus verticalis ateralbus Salomonsen, 1964- Djaul Island
The Djaul monarch was earlier considered a full species but is now considered a subspecies based on plumage, vocal and genetic evidence.


Description
This species is a pied bird with a white face and black throat. The male has a black crown with a band of white and a black tail and upperparts. The legs are dark grey or blackish and the bill is grey in colour.
The male and female are similar, except that the female has a grey back and rump. The juvenile has a pale grey forehead and face. 
The subspecies ateralbus is larger than the nominate and has extensive white on the outer tail feathers.


Voice
The song is a descending series of thin fine whistles.


Breeding
It usually breeds from August to January. The nest is cup-shaped, made of plant fibers and lichens and joined together with spider webs placed at a height of 1.5 meters. The clutch is usually 2 eggs, which are pale pinkish with black spots and are approximately 24 x 17 millimeters.


Habitats and habits
It is usually found in semi-evergreen forests and bamboo thickets at an elevation of 1300 meters.
It is usually solitary and sometimes occurs in mixed-species flocks in pursuit of its insect prey.


References

The '''Bismarck monarch''' ('''''Symposiachrus verticalis'''''), formerly known as the '''black-tailed monarch''', is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Monarchidae]]. It is [[Endemism|endemic]] to the [[Bismarck Archipelago]] of Papua New Guinea. Its natural [[habitat]] is subtropical or tropical moist lowland [[forest]]s.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Clement |first=Peter |last2=del Hoyo |first2=Josep |last3=Christie |first3=David |last4=Collar |first4=Nigel |date=2020 |title=Bismarck Monarch (Symposiachrus verticalis), version 1.0 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/bltmon1/cur/introduction |journal=Birds of the World |language=en |doi=10.2173/bow.bltmon1.01 |issn=2771-3105|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

==Taxonomy and systematics==
This species was originally placed in the genus ''[[Monarcha]]'' until moved to ''Symposiachrus'' in 2009.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.worldbirdnames.org/Archives/Master_IOC_list_v2.0.xls|title=IOC Bird List v2.0|date=2009|access-date=28 October 2016}}</ref><ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen | date=August 2024 | title=Monarchs | work=IOC World Bird List Version 14.2 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/monarchs/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=3 September 2024 }}</ref>

Two subspecies are recognized-

* '''Black-tailed monarch''', ''Symposiachrus verticalis verticalis'' <small>Sclater, 1877</small>- [[Bismarck Archipelago]]
* '''Djaul monarch''', ''Symposiachrus verticalis ateralbus'' <small>Salomonsen, 1964</small>- [[Dyaul Island|Djaul Island]]

The Djaul monarch was earlier considered a full species but is now considered a subspecies based on plumage, vocal and genetic evidence.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Andersen |first=Michael J. |last2=Hosner |first2=Peter A. |last3=Filardi |first3=Christopher E. |last4=Moyle |first4=Robert G. |date=2015 |title=Phylogeny of the monarch flycatchers reveals extensive paraphyly and novel relationships within a major Australo-Pacific radiation |url=https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ympev.2014.11.010 |journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution |volume=83 |pages=118–136 |doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2014.11.010 |issn=1055-7903|url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Home |url=http://www.avilist.org/ |access-date=2026-03-27 |website=AviList: The Global Avian Checklist |language=en-US}}</ref>

== Description ==
This species is a pied bird with a white face and black throat. The male has a black crown with a band of white and a black tail and upperparts. The legs are dark grey or blackish and the bill is grey in colour.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book |url=http://archive.org/details/handbookofbirdso0011unse |title=Handbook of the birds of the world |date=1992 |publisher=Barcelona : Lynx Edicions |others=Internet Archive |isbn=978-84-87334-10-8}}</ref>

The male and female are similar, except that the female has a grey back and rump. The juvenile has a pale grey forehead and face.<ref name=":0" /> 

The subspecies ''ateralbus'' is larger than the nominate and has extensive white on the outer tail feathers.<ref name=":0" />

== Voice ==
The song is a descending series of thin fine whistles.<ref name=":0" />

== Breeding ==
It usually breeds from August to January. The nest is cup-shaped, made of plant fibers and lichens and joined together with spider webs placed at a height of 1.5 meters. The clutch is usually 2 eggs, which are pale pinkish with black spots and are approximately 24 x 17 millimeters.<ref name=":0" />

== Habitats and habits ==
It is usually found in semi-evergreen forests and bamboo thickets at an elevation of 1300 meters.<ref name=":0" />

It is usually solitary and sometimes occurs in mixed-species flocks in pursuit of its insect prey.<ref name=":0" />
